MATRIX FIST
Player Card

Complete rules, safety guidelines, scoring system, and filming instructions.
Read before your first match. Share before every challenge.

Kids ≤12: First to 5
Adults 13+: First to 10
Ages 10+ · Patent Pending
📋 Official Player Card — Print & Share

Save this page, print the Player Card, or share the link before every match. Every player should read the rules before their first game.

💡
Print Tip
Use your browser's Print function (Ctrl+P) to save as PDF or print. The nav and footer are hidden automatically when printing.
Section 1.1

Setup & Stance

Step 1
The Vitruvian Standard
Stand upright and balanced — arms at natural extension, feet shoulder-width apart. Model your stance on Leonardo da Vinci's Vitruvian Man. No crouching, leaning, or overextending. The body is your anchor. The arm is your instrument.
Step 2
Side by Side
Stand shoulder to shoulder, facing opposite directions — like two guards at a post. Height difference is noted but not disqualifying. Extreme mismatch not recommended.
Step 3
Set the Distance
Reach across and grip each other's elbow to measure safe reach. The taller or longer-reaching player must not extend past the opponent's elbow. Release — that's your play zone. Fathers playing with children: taller player self-limits reach to the child's elbow.
Step 4
Eyes Forward. Always.
No looking sideways. Peripheral sense only. This is not a game of vision — it is a game of feel. The arm knows before the eye does. Stay zen, like a kung fu master.
Step 5 — Mandatory
Unused Hand Behind Back
Place your unused hand firmly behind your back. One arm in play. Always. Back of the hand must remain touching the lower back.
Step 6
The Countdown & Elbow Grip
Count down: 3 — 2 — 1 — BEGIN. If gripping each other's elbow at zero, release to start. The elbow grip is a mark of sportsmanship — like the boxing glove tap or the martial arts bow. Encouraged, not required. Rooted in the traditional elbow handshake.
⚠️ 1st Infraction
Unused hand leaves back — Verbal warning. Match continues.
🚫 2nd Infraction
Unused hand leaves back again — Point removed. No further warnings.
Section 1.2

Scoring & Play

Valid Grip Zones
Forearm · Wrist · Hand · Fingers
All are valid scoring zones. Hold for one full second = point. Call your running score aloud — "1", "2", "3"... up to "WIN" at 5 or 10. Release immediately after calling.
The Gold Standard
Gentle Control Wins
The most sought-after playing style. Calm grip. Relaxed hold. Minimal force. A Master who holds through a yank with quiet stillness is more formidable than one who grabs hard.
Absolute Rule
No Pulling — Ever
You may grip and hold — you may never drag, haul, or pull your opponent's arm toward you. Pulling = immediate point forfeit. A grip is a grip. Not a tug.
The Escape
The Yank
A gripped player may yank sharply once to break free before the one-second hold completes. One yank per grab. A true Master maintains grip through the yank. Legend holds that the greatest Grand Masters survived two or even three yanks and still held.
5
First to Win
Players aged 12 and under. 5-minute round limit.
10
First to Win
Players aged 13 and over. 10-minute round limit.
1 sec
To Score a Point
Hold for one full second. Call your score. Release immediately.
1
Yank Per Grab
One escape yank permitted. Masters hold through it.
Section 2.1

Fairness Check & Safety

Run this check before every match. Both players must be satisfied before the countdown begins.

✅
Before Every Match
  • Fingernails — trim or file. Both players check each other.
  • Sleeves — long sleeves recommended. Bare forearms accepted with mutual acknowledgement.
  • Space — minimum 3 feet clear on all sides. No furniture limiting arm motion.
  • Prior injuries — any arm, wrist, elbow, or shoulder injury: do not play.
  • Height/reach disparity — taller player self-limits to opponent's elbow.
  • Win/loss records — extreme skill mismatch must be acknowledged.
  • Warm up — wrist rolls, arm circles, finger spreads before first match.
  • Mutual agreement — both players confirm ready and understand the rules.
🧘
The Matrix Fist Mindset
  • Be zen. Calm, focused, and silent.
  • No trash talk or taunting. The hands speak.
  • Call your own scores honestly. Integrity is your rank.
  • Disputed call? Replay the round. No arguments.
  • Switch arms between rounds — mandatory.
  • Shake hands after every round — win or lose.
🚫
Stop & Do Not Play If...
  • Any arm, wrist, elbow, or shoulder injury is present.
  • Either player feels pain or discomfort — stop immediately.
  • Players under 13 without an adult present.
  • Fingernails have not been checked by both players.
  • Reach disparity is extreme and the taller player won't self-limit.
The Stance

The Vitruvian Standard

The official Matrix Fist stance is modelled on Leonardo da Vinci's Vitruvian Man — the body as geometry, movement as mathematics. The figure below demonstrates the extended arm positions and grip range used in competitive play.

How to Film

Film It Right

Camera 1 — Required
Face Player 1
Faces Player 1 directly. Captures their face, active arm, and the opponent's hand in frame. Portrait orientation for TikTok and Reels.
Camera 2 — Required
Face Player 2
Mirrors Camera 1 from the opposite side. Both cameras running simultaneously gives you the split-screen edit that shows both reactions at the moment of the grab.
Camera 3 — The Money Shot
Overhead · Slow Motion
Directly overhead looking down at the fist action. Use slow motion. This is the cinematic shot — the grab highlight from above. The frame that goes viral.
End Every Video
The Challenge Call-Out
End every video with the challenge — "My Matrix Fist is ready. [Name], step up." Tag your next opponent by name on screen. This is how the chain grows.
"My Matrix Fist is ready. [Name], step up."
↩ "Matrix Fist accepted. Booth or bare — your call."
